coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot-assisted synthesis of gold nanoparticles for the development of a detection platform for trichlorfon
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Murugesan, Pramila
Moses, J.A.
coredata.dc:description: Concerns over environmental pollution and human and animal health are on the rise, with insecticide/pesticide residues posing a significant challenge. Rapid and sensitive insecticide/pesticide detection systems play a crucial role in ensuring compliance with regulatory standards. Owing to their unique physicochemical features, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) stand out as an excellent platform for analyte detection with appreciable accuracy. In this study, a low-cost precursor material was used to synthesize hydrophilic functional groups containing carbon dots (CD) and then used as a stabilizing and reducing agent for synthesizing AuNPs via a one-step process. Various microscopic and spectroscopic techniques were used to characterize the material; sphere-shaped AuNPs formation was confirmed on the CD's surface. The fabricated nanoparticle was used for colorimetric sensing of trichlorfon in an aqueous solution. The quantification limit and detection limit were determined to be 9.887 µM and 29.96 µM, respectively. This study confirms that the developed AuNPs-based sensing platform offers a powerful and versatile system for real-time, on-site insecticide monitoring, with a range of futuristic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Gold nanoparticles
Nanosensing
Pesticide residue
Trichlorfon

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection performance of AuNPs/CDs for pesticide residues

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Phase structures of CDs and AuNPs/ CDs; (b) Functional group analysis of CDs and AuNPs/ CDs.
XPS spectra of (a) CDs (survey); deconvoluted XPS spectra of (b) C1s and (c) N1s and (d) O1s; (e) AuNPs/C-dots (survey); deconvoluted XPS spectra of (f) C1s and (g) N1s and (h) O1s.
EDAX results for (a) CDs and (b) AuNPs/CDs; EDX mapping for (c) CDs and (d) AuNPs/CDs.
HRTEM image of (a), (b) CDs; (d), (e) AuNPs/CDs; (c), (f) SAED pattern of (c) CDs, (f) AuNPs/CDs (inset: particle size distribution).
(a) UV vis spectra; (b) Tauc plot; (c) extinction coefficient and linear refractive index of CDs and AuNPs/CDs.
Zeta potential of (a) CDs and (b) AuNPs/CDs.

(a) Detection of pesticide residues (concentration: 100 µM) in an aqueous solution using AuNPs/CDs; (b) FTIR spectra of AuNPs/CDs after addition of trichlorfon pesticide; (c) Influence of incubation time on the trichlorfon (concentration: 100 µM) detection; (d) Influence of AuNPs/C-dots volume on the trichlorfon (concentration: 100 µM) detection.

(a) Effect of trichlorfon concentration on the detection performance of AuNPs/CDs (Inset: color changes with and without pesticide); (b) Linear plot of trichlorfon residue detection at different concentrations.
Comparisons of the performance of the different types of nanomaterials for the detection of trichlorfon.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot-assisted synthesis of gold nanoparticles for the development of a detection platform for trichlorfon

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Computational Modeling and Nanoscale Processing Unit, Department of Food Process Engineering, National Institute of Food Technology, Entrepreneurship and Management, Thanjavur, Ministry of Food Processing Industries, Government of India, Thanjavur - 613005, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Concerns over environmental pollution and human and animal health are on the rise, with insecticide/pesticide residues posing a significant challenge. Rapid and sensitive insecticide/pesticide detection systems play a crucial role in ensuring compliance with regulatory standards. Owing to their unique physicochemical features, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) stand out as an excellent platform for analyte detection with appreciable accuracy. In this study, a low-cost precursor material was used to synthesize hydrophilic functional groups containing carbon dots (CD) and then used as a stabilizing and reducing agent for synthesizing AuNPs via a one-step process. Various microscopic and spectroscopic techniques were used to characterize the material; sphere-shaped AuNPs formation was confirmed on the CD's surface. The fabricated nanoparticle was used for colorimetric sensing of trichlorfon in an aqueous solution. The quantification limit and detection limit were determined to be 9.887 µM and 29.96 µM, respectively. This study confirms that the developed AuNPs-based sensing platform offers a powerful and versatile system for real-time, on-site insecticide monitoring, with a range of futuristic applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All relevant data from the study are available within the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The extensive application of organophosphorus insecticides in modern agriculture has dramatically increased crop yields, but the persistence and accumulation of these chemicals in food and the environment pose serious threats to ecological balance and human health [1]. Particularly concerning among them is trichlorfon, a widely used insecticide that can potentially be neurotoxic, carcinogenic, and break down into the more dangerous chemical dichlorvos [2]. Recent monitoring reports indicate that up to 25 % of the agricultural products evaluated had trichlorfon residues, often exceeding the maximum residue limits (MRLs) set by regulatory agencies. This underscores an urgent need for effective detecting tools to ensure food safety and environmental protection. Traditional detection routes, including chromatographic routes, provide high accuracy but are limited by their high operational costs, complex procedures, and the need for skilled technicians. These drawbacks have prompted a shift toward nano-sensor-based technologies, which offer low-cost, rapid, and sensitive alternatives suitable for real-time applications [3].

Recent advances in nanotechnology have enabled the development of highly sensitive nanosensors utilizing materials such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), graphene oxide, and carbon quantum dots [4,5]. These materials enhance detection performance through improved electrical conductivity, surface reactivity, and signal amplification. Despite these advancements, few nanosensors have been tailored specifically for trichlorfon detection, and many existing sensors still face challenges such as limited selectivity, environmental instability, and complicated synthesis routes. Over the past years, noble metal nanostructure-based nano-sensors have garnered much attention in sensing various analytes, including heavy metals, pesticides, pharmaceutical molecules and food adulterants [6]. Owing to their fascinating properties, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have emerged as one of the most popular sensing platforms, amongst other noble metals such as platinum (Pt), palladium (Pd) and silver (Ag). Nevertheless, the rapid aggregation of AuNPs could hinder its sensing efficiency [7,8]. To address such limitations, there is a strong focus on developing efficient methods to create size and shape-controlled Au nanostructures with enhanced stability. Generally, non-green reducing agents (hydrazine, sodium borohydride, polyethylene glycol, and citrate) and capping agents (polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), oleic acid, thiol, and cetyl-trimethylammonium bromide) have been employed for synthesizing AuNPs. However, these are detrimental to the health and environment and are often expensive [9–11]. Moreover, traditional chemical reduction approaches could limit their environmental sustainability and scalability. Therefore, to produce AuNPs with regulated shape and size, there is a quest to optimize novel processes that are effective, surfactant-free, economical, and sustainable.

Typically, functional groups containing carbonaceous raw material-derived fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) have multiple hydroxyl, epoxy, carbonyl and carboxyl-containing functional groups with narrow dimensions (less than 10 nm) and can perform as an outstanding electron donor and electron acceptor, reducing compounds for the formation of metal NPs from metal salts [12,13]. For instance, the reduced state of CDs could act as a stabilizer and reductant for the AuNPs formation from Au3 +, and this can reduce fabrication and energy costs [11]. In a previous study, it was demonstrated that the existence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on carbon quantum dot surfaces could stabilize silver NPs [14]. In another study, coal-derived CDs showed significant potential to reduce Ag ions to the AgNPs, which showed antimicrobial activity towards Rhodococcus soli bacterial strains [15]. Further, plant (for example, Gynostemma) derived CDs were reported to act as an active reducing agent for the formation of AgNP-based nanocomposite [16]. Given the capabilities of CDs to control the shape of nanomaterials, research into novel green techniques to produce AuNPs is highly desired. In contrast, the CD-assisted synthesis in this study eliminates the need for external capping agents, offering a simpler, greener, and more cost-effective alternative. The high colloidal stability of CD-stabilized AuNPs further highlights the advantages of this approach [17]. Moreover, the synthesis occurs under mild, aqueous, and ambient conditions, offering significant advantages. However, until now, no studies have reported CDs-derived AuNPs for the detection of trichlorfon pesticide residue.

This work reported the adaptation of a facile route to synthesize morphologically structure-controllable AuNPs using biopolymer-derived carbon quantum dots by simple mixing of gold auric chloride with CDs. Then, the physicochemical features of the obtained AuNPs hybridized carbon quantum dot NPs were characterized using microscopic and spectroscopic techniques. Finally, the synthesized AuNPs/CDs were found to be an excellent sensing platform for insecticide/pesticide residue detection.

In this investigation, the following analytical-grade compounds were used: tetra chloroauric (III) acid trihydrate (HAuCl4.3H2O, MW: 339.785 g/mol), urea (CH₄N₂O MW 60.06 g/mol), trisodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7; MW 258.06 g/mol), citric acid (C₆H₈O₇ MW 192.124 g/mol), imidacloprid (C9H10ClN5O2), chlorpyrifos (C9H11Cl3NO3PS), trichlorfon (C4H8Cl3O4P) and atrazine (C8H14ClN5) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, United States.

A hydrothermal route was used for the preparation of CDs. In short, a mixture of distilled water (45 mL) mixed with urea (1.513 g) and citric acid (2.545 g) was first prepared. A hydrothermal reactor was then used to heat the solution for six hours at 200°C before letting it cool. Then, contaminants from the sample were eliminated by a 0.45 µm nylon filter. For 12 h, the CD sample was dialyzed via a dialysis bag using deionized water. The resulting CDs were used for further examination after being freeze-dried.

Tetra chloroauric (III) acid trihydrate (1 mM, 50 mL) and CDs (1000 µL) were mixed and subjected to 30 min of stirring. Following the dropwise addition of 1000 µL of trisodium citrate (38.8 mM), the sample was then stirred for 2 h at 37ºC. Finally, the samples were filtered and stored for further use.

A multimode microplate reader (Spectra Max iD3; USA) was used to evaluate the optical features of CDs and AuNPs/CDs samples. The valance band potential (E
VB
), conduction band potential (E
CB
) and energy band gap (E
g ) were determined using Eqs. (1) to (3) [18,19].

Then, Eqs. (4) to (7) were adapted to evaluate the optical characteristics [12,20].

Where, A, t, λ, K, and R are the absorbance of the sample, the thickness of the sample, the irradiation source wavelength, linear refractive index, the extinction coefficient, and reflectance, respectively.

The chemical composition was identified via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS; Thermo Fisher Scientific, United States), energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDAX; Tecnai G2 F20; FEI, Netherlands) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR; Thermo Nicolet Nexus 6700, United States) spectroscopy. The crystalline phase as well as the average crystalline size of both samples were measured using CuKα radiation using a X’Pert3 Powder diffractometer (ʎ=1.5418 Å) (Malvern Panalytical; UK). Using the Debye-Scherrer equation, the average crystalline size (D) of the AuNPs was measured (Eq. 8) [21].
(8)
D =
K ʎ
β COS θ
Where, θ, β, k, and ʎ are the Bragg angle, full-width half maximum, Scherrer constant (0.9–1), and wavelength, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection performance of AuNPs/CDs for pesticide residues
ce:section.ce:para:
Different pesticides including pesticides (chlorpyrifos, trichlorfon, atrazine, and imidacloprid) were chosen for this work. Pesticide (100 µM) and AuNPs/C-dots were added together for the detection study. A multimode microplate reader was used to measure absorbance (230–800 nm wavelength range) following 20 min of incubation at 37°C. The detection limit and quantification were measured using Eq. 9 and 10 [22].
(9)
Limit of detection =
3 * Standard deviation
Slope
(10)
Limit of quantification =
10 * standard
deviation
Slope

Considering results observed in preliminary experiments, the viability and suitability of developed AuNPs/CDs were used for trichlorfon pesticide detection in tomato samples. Using distilled water, the tomato sample was cleaned, chopped, and then ground. Subsequently, distilled water (25 mL) was used to filter the resultant sample, and the volume was adjusted to 100 mL. Trichlorfon (20 µM) was spiked onto the prepared sample and the absorption intensity was measured.
The crystalline structure, morphological structure, chemical composition and surface charge were determined using different analytical techniques.

The XRD spectra for CDs are shown in Fig. 1(a). The dominant broad peak observed at 21.39º (002) indicates the amorphous structure with a 0.428 nm D-spacing value. Notably, the D-spacing value is larger than that of bulk graphite, which indicates the formation of a disordered graphitic core structure [13]. The carbon lattice structure only was detected in the CDs, according to the XRD data, explaining that additional contaminants in the CDs had been eliminated. The clear peaks for AuNPs/CDs at 37.98º, 44.26º, 64.39º, and 78.1º are indicated by the diffraction from the (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes of face-centered cubic (fcc) phase of gold crystals, respectively, also demonstrate the formation of Au nanoparticle [23].

According to Eq. 10, the crystallite size for AuNPs was calculated to be 46.64 nm. Owing to interactions among CDs and AuNPs, the spectrum matched to the plane (002) and was observed to be shifted to 22.35° from 21.39°.

FTIR spectra were obtained to understand the chemical groups found on the CDs surface (Fig. 1(b)). The transmittance values for CDs showed peaks at 3243.73 cm−1, 1635.48 cm−1, 1560.26 cm−1 and 1402.68 cm−1, corresponding to OH, CO, N-H and CC bending, respectively [24]. In the case of AuNPs/CDs, only two peaks were observed (3254.19 cm−1 and 1632.64 cm−1), indicative of O-H as well as CO groups, respectively [24,25]. FTIR results indicated that an enormous quantity of COOH and O-H groups were connected to the surface of the prepared CDs. The hydrophilicity and water solubility of the CDs may be ascribed to the appearance of these bonds. The identification of these bonds and functional groups suggested that several intricate processes, including polycondensation, intermolecular cyclization, and material breakdown, took place during the synthesis of CDs [26].

The chemical state of the samples was evaluated using XPS spectroscopy. The presence of C, N and O elements is shown in the XPS survey spectra ( Fig. 2(a) and Fig (e)), and it is evident that CDs have only a few N atoms present. Fig. 2(b) and Fig. 2(f) represent the high-resolution XPS spectra of the CDs and AuNPs/CDs. Three distinct bonding environments that corresponded to C atoms were exposed by the C1s spectra of the materials including 284.82 eV (CC/C-C), 286.56 eV (CO) and 288.56 eV (O-CO) [12]. Two different small peaks were seen in the N1s spectra including 399.01 eV (pyrrolic species) and 400.51 eV (graphitic N species) [21] (Fig. 2(c) and Fig. 3(g)). In addition, two prominent peaks were observed between 532.45 eV to 533.55 eV corresponding to the C–O or OH moieties on its surface [27] (Fig. 2(d) and Fig. 3(h)). These findings verify that carboxylic/carbonyl moieties, hydroxyl and amine that originate from their precursor were used to functionalize the synthesized CDs. The survey spectra for AuNPs-CDs clearly confirm the presence of Au in different states with a minimal amount. Moreover, the weight percentage of C, H, N and O was found to using elemental analysis also proved the occurrence of C (97.15 %), N (1.10 %) and O (1.65 %) in CDs. Also, AuNPs-CDs are composed of C (96.78 %), O (1.09 %), N (1.00 %), and Au (1.13 %) (Fig. 3(a) and Fig. 3(b)). The presence of nitrogen is primarily attributed to the incorporation of nitrogen-containing precursor (urea) during the synthesis process. Nitrogen doping not only modifies the surface chemistry but also introduces additional active sites that can facilitate metal ion binding and electron transfer processes [28]. In this context, the nitrogen atoms in the form of amine or amide could enhance the interaction between CDs and Au³ ⁺, supporting the reduction and stabilization of AuNPs [29].

The HRTEM image of the obtained CDs and AuNPs/CDs is illustrated in Fig. 4. Monodispersed CDs were found to be spherical, with an average size of ∼3.34 nm (Fig. 4(a-b)). Fig. 4(c) (inset) displays the amorphous character of CDs as confirmed by their selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern. An interplanar spacing of 0.2 nm was found in CDs (Inset: Fig. 4(b)). The synthesized AuNPs showed highly ordered planar spacing with the decahedral structure in their HRTEM images (Fig. 4(d-e)). The distinctive lattice spacing value was calculated to be 0.204 nm (200), which reveals the fcc structure of AuNPs formed under these conditions. It can be seen that both CDs and AuNPs are intercalated to each other. As shown by the size distribution histogram, the particle size varies between 3 and 10 nm (inset: Fig. 4(f)). The decahedral shape of the AuNPs confirms the polycrystalline nature of AuNPs formed.

The optical features of CDs and AuNPs/CDs are illustrated in Fig. 5(a). The peaks were observed at 298 nm and 437 nm for CDs. The absorption band 298 nm is ascribed to the n-π* electronic CO group transition. Notably, the absorption band is drastically decreased in AuNPs/CDs composite, which indicates that the CDs perform as a stabilizing and reducing compound towards the fabrication of AuNPs/CDs. Another peak at 421 nm in CDs is indexed to electron transition of n → π* for CN. The energy band gap of CDs and AuNPs/CDs were found to be 2.35 eV and 2.92 eV, respectively (Fig. 5(b)). The conduction as well as the valance band values of CDs and AuNPs-CDs were determined to be 1.435 eV and 0.176 eV and 3.705 eV and 3.156 eV, respectively. The extinction coefficient and linear refractive index of both samples are represented in Fig. 5(c).

Furthermore, a zeta potential analysis was performed to ascertain colloidal stability as well as electrical charges on the NP’s surface. Fig. 6 presents the zeta potential measurement of CDs and AuNPs/CDs. Higher negative zeta potential values of −20.7 and −29.2 mV were observed for CDs and AuNPs/CDs, respectively, explaining the appearance of carboxy and hydroxyl negatively charged groups on the surface [30]. Also, higher negative zeta potential values indicate that the synthesized NPs have good colloidal stability. The stability of the NP against aggregation can be explained by the negative value. Furthermore, the negative value of the AuNPs could be caused by the capping effect of CDs [22]. Moreover, strongly anionic nanomaterials have zeta potentials smaller than −30 mV [30].

Owing to their optical characteristics CDs and AuNPs were tested for the detection of various pesticides including atrazine, chlorpyrifos, trichlorfon, and imidacloprid. For pesticide quantification, the UV absorption spectra were analyzed ( Fig. 7(a)). After mixing pesticides with CDs, no absorption spectrum was observed, indicating that the prepared CDs do not exhibit sensitivity toward pesticide detection. However, when a pesticide was added to the AuNPs/CDs sample, the absorption intensity (the binding ability) of AuNPs/CDs got diminished in order of trichlorfon>atrazine>imidacloprid>chlorpyrifos. Thus, the observation revealed that AuNPs/CDs-based sensing platform displayed the highest detection performance for trichlorfon than the other tested counterparts. The interaction between phosphorus groups and the gold surface is responsible for the high binding affinity between AuNPs and phosphorus-containing trichlorfon [31]. Previous research has indicated that compounds containing phosphonate or bisphosphonate groups strongly favor attaching to gold surfaces. Bisphosphonate-functionalized AuNPs, for example, demonstrated the fastest binding kinetics and the highest binding affinity, followed by phosphonate-functionalized AuNPs [32]. Notably, a new characteristic peak is found in FTIR spectra after the addition of trichlorfon pesticide with AuNPs/CDs, which confirms the presence of P-O/P-O-C group with the wavenumber of 1016.48 cm−1 (Fig. 7(b)). This implies that phosphoryl oxygen groups (P = O) containing trichlorfon can interact strongly with gold surfaces to increase binding affinity and form Au–O–P bonds [33]. Furthermore, the sensitivity of the system may be enhanced by hydrogen bonding, electrostatic attraction, and even π–π stacking with trichlorfon, enabled by the distinct surface chemistry of the CDs, which includes amine, hydroxyl, and carboxyl functional groups. The behavior of optical response may also be influenced by these interactions, suggesting the presence of other signal transduction pathways as energy or electron transfer [34].

Different influencing factors, including incubation time, solution volume, and trichlorfon concentration, could alter the detection performance. Fig. 7(c) represents the influence of the incubation period for trichlorfon pesticide detection. It was found that the absorption intensity was diminished from 2.9432 to 1.947 for the first 20 min. Afterward, the absorption intensity increased with time, indicating that sufficient pesticide had bound to the surface of AuNPs, and the system had reached equilibrium [35]. The impact of AuNPs/CDs volumes (100–500 µL) on trichlorfon detection was examined (Fig. 7(d)). The sensing responsiveness improved with a decreasing volume of AuNPs/CDs. The absorption intensity was found to be at 0.1387, 0.2943, 0.4027, 0.3716 and 0.3847 for 100, 200, 300, 400 and 500 µL, respectively. Smaller volumes of AuNPs/CDs displayed a higher surface area relative to their volume, providing more active sites for pesticide interaction. This increased availability of binding sites facilitates greater analyte adsorption, enhancing detection sensitivity [36]. After 20 min of incubation, the UV–vis spectra for various pesticide solution concentrations containing AuNPs/CDs were recorded and are displayed in Fig. 8(a). When trichlorfon was added, the intensity of the AuNPs/CDs-based pesticide sensor decreased from 10 µM to 100 µM. The diminishing intensity is mainly due to a combination of quenching mechanisms, AuNP aggregation, inner filter effects, and charge transfer interactions [37]. The color changes of the reaction solution with and without trichlorfon was incorporated in the revised manuscript (Fig. 8(a) (inset)). Additionally, an additional quantitative clarification of the sensing performance was obtained by graphing the calibration curve (Fig. 8(b)). The plot displays a linear response (linear equation: y = 0.56225 x + 0.00406), with an R2 value of 0.98209. It was determined that the LOD was 9.887 µM and the LOQ was 29.96 µM.

The feasibility of this route for the detection of trichlorfon was studied in a real sample (tomato). It was found that the recovery percentage was 96.05 %. Compared to conventional techniques such as gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), the developed CDs/AuNPs-based sensor offers distinct advantages in terms of speed, simplicity, and portability. In contrast, the CDs/AuNPs nano sensing platform enables rapid (20 min) and sensitive detection with an LOD of 9.887 µM. The sensing platform provides a visible color change and simultaneous reduction in optical intensity that enhances the analytic readability. Moreover, its operation under neutral pH, absence of enzymes or hazardous solvents, and low material cost make it highly suitable for on-site or point-of-care environmental monitoring. Overall, the method balances sensitivity, selectivity, cost-efficiency, and portability, positioning it as a compelling alternative to traditional techniques for rapid pesticide residue analysis. The sensitivity and selectivity of various previously reported nano-based sensing platforms for the sensing of trichlorfon pesticides are related and illustrated in Table 1.

In summary, the work demonstrated the sensing performance of AuNPs/CDs towards pesticide detection. Increased colloidal stability and environmental stability may be exhibited by the CDs. The synthesized CDs were employed to stabilize formation. Different spectroscopic and microscopic techniques were adapted for evaluating the physico-chemical features of AuNPs/CDs. All analytical studies reveal that only a limited amount of AuNPs was intercalated on the surface of CDs. The detection limit for trichlorfon pesticide was found to be at 9.887 µM. Moreover, the detection performance of AuNPs/CDs was tested towards the trichlorfon pesticide in tomatoes. The percentage of recovery was observed to be around 95.5 %. Overall, the study reveals that metal-free CDs could act as an effective stabilizing and green reducing agent for the formation of AuNPs, which act as a sensor for pesticide residue detection. That is, the current study demonstrates metal free abundant surface functional group CDs act as a green reduction and stabilizing compound for metal NP formation and optical-based pesticide detection application. Surface modifications using nanozyme and ratiometric dual mode detections are possible strategies that could further enhance the sensitivity of AuNPs/CDs.
Conceptualization, Methodology: Pramila M. and J. A. Moses; Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - Original Draft: Pramila M.; Writing - Review & Editing and Supervision: J. A. Moses.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The first author thanks the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), Government of India for providing fellowship under the N-PDF scheme (PDF/2021/002639).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Unraveling the complexity of organophosphorus pesticides: ecological risks, biochemical pathways and the promise of machine learning
Sci. Total Environ.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Dong, Y. Zhu, R. Che, T. Chen, J. Liang, M. Xia, F. Wang, Unraveling the complexity of organophosphorus pesticides: Ecological risks, biochemical pathways and the promise of machine learning, Sci. Total Environ. 974 (2025) 179206. https://doi.org/https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2025.179206.

J. Food Compos. Anal.

Wang, Seasonal variation and exposure risk assessment of pesticide residues in vegetables from Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region of China during 2010–24, J. Food Compos. Anal. 58 (2017) 1–9. https://doi.org/https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jfca.2016.12.025.

Nanoscale Res. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Aykaç, H. Gergeroglu, B. Beşli, E.Ö. Akkaş, A. Yavaş, S. Güler, F. Güneş, M. Erol, An Overview on Recent Progress of Metal Oxide/Graphene/CNTs-Based Nanobiosensors, Nanoscale Res. Lett. 16 (2021) 65. https://doi.org/10.1186/s11671-021-03519-w.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dot nanocomposites: electroanalytical and optical sensor technology perspective
J. Anal. Sci. Technol.
